summary: Gruber argues that news publications must be owned by people whose primary commitment is journalism itself, not by wealthy dilettantes or entertainment conglomerates for whom news is a secondary concern. He uses the Philadelphia Inquirer's decline under profit-focused Knight Ridder, Disney's cowardly settlement of Trump's defamation suit against ABC News, Bezos's suppression of the Washington Post's Harris endorsement, and the LA Times owner's pro-Trump meddling as evidence. He contrasts these failures with the Ochs-Sulzberger family's stewardship of the New York Times and even Rupert Murdoch's genuine commitment to his news empire's mission, however pernicious. He endorses Kara Swisher's bid to buy the Post, invoking Katharine Graham's courageous decision to publish the Pentagon Papers as the gold standard of ownership commitment.
key_points: News organizations need owners whose primary mission is journalism — not profit, entertainment, or avoiding political retaliation

Disney's settlement of Trump's defamation suit against Stephanopoulos made business sense for Disney but devastated ABC News's credibility, illustrating the fundamental conflict of entertainment companies owning journalism
Bezos, the LA Times' Soon-Shiong, and Disney all failed journalism in different ways when confronted by Trump's power — cowardice and appeasement corrode editorial independence
Even Rupert Murdoch, despite his pernicious influence, exemplifies committed news ownership by keeping his media empire as his life's work rather than selling it off
Katharine Graham's Pentagon Papers decision remains the ultimate test of ownership commitment — risking prison and financial ruin for the mission of journalism
money_quotes: Journalism is not one of those businesses. Profits are important because profits maintain independence and pay for talent. But independence is more important than anything, and there can be no true independence for a publication when the owner is not committed to the cause.

Instructing a daily newspaper's editorial board to take a break from writing about the incoming president of the United States is like telling a bar to take a break from selling beer. It's the entire point of the establishment.
Disney would fight tooth and nail to defend Mickey Mouse, but George Stephanopoulos's face isn't printed on t-shirts at Disneyland.

It's easy to be a good owner of a good news outlet when times are normal. It's when times are hard, whether financially, or more crucially, when truth speaks to malicious power, that it becomes essential for the owner to be in the game first and foremost for the mission of journalism itself.
Like a don's consigliere, a news publication in conflict with malicious power needs an owner with the stomach for war.

Katharine Graham 'took a big gulp and said, Let's go. Let's publish.' Jeff Bezos didn't even have the intestinal fortitude to allow the Post editorial board to publish an utterly unsurprising election endorsement.

He's singlehandedly responsible for more of what's gone wrong in the United States over the last quarter century than any person alive, including Donald Trump, who I believe would never have even run for president, let alone become president twice, without the deeply pernicious and pervasive influence of Fox News.
It's obvious Bezos does care about the Post. But it's also now obvious that he does not care nearly enough.
key_insight: Journalism can only maintain its independence when owned by people for whom the mission of journalism itself — not profit, entertainment, or political self-preservation — is the paramount concern.
